tex:
Marianne sells photos.

(a) The selling price of each photo is \$6.

(i) (b) The selling price for each photo is made up of two parts, printing cost and profit.\\
For each photo, the ratio printing cost : profit = 5 : 3.

Calculate the profit she makes on each photo.

(c) Calculate her profit as a percentage of the selling price.

(d) Calculate the selling price of a photo in euros (€) when the exchange rate is €1 = \$1.091 .

(e) Marianne sells two sizes of photo.\\
These photos are mathematically similar rectangles.\\
The smaller photo has length 15 cm and width 12 cm.\\
The larger photo has area 352.8 cm$^2$.

Calculate the length of the larger photo.

(f) In a sale, Marianne buys a new camera for \$483.\\
This is a reduction of 8\% on the original price.

Calculate the original price of the camera.

tex:
A school nurse records the height, h cm, of each of 180 children.
The table shows the information.

\begin{tabular}{l|cccccc}
Height (h cm) & 60 ≤ h < 70 & 70 ≤ h < 90 & 90 ≤ h < 100 & 100 ≤ h < 110 & 110 ≤ h < 115 & 115 ≤ h < 125 \\
\hline
Frequency & 8 & 26 & 35 & 67 & 28 & 16
\end{tabular}

(a) Calculate an estimate of the mean.
Give your answer correct to 1 decimal place.

(b) In a histogram showing the information, the height of the bar for the interval 60 ≤ h < 70 is 0.4 cm.

Calculate the height of the bar for each of the following intervals.

115 ≤ h < 125  cm

110 ≤ h < 115  cm

70 ≤ h < 90  cm

(c) Complete the cumulative frequency table below.

\begin{tabular}{l|cccccc}
Height (h cm) & h < 70 & h < 90 & h < 100 & h < 110 & h < 115 & h < 125 \\
\hline
Cumulative frequency &  &  &  &  &  & 180
\end{tabular}

(d) On the grid opposite, draw a cumulative frequency diagram.

%\begin{InsertScreenShot}
%    
%\end{InsertScreenShot}

(e) Use your cumulative frequency diagram to find an estimate of

(i) (f) the interquartile range,

(g) the 70th percentile,

(h) the number of children with height greater than 106 cm.

Paulo and Jim each buy sacks of rice but from different shops.
Paulo pays \$72 for sacks costing \$m each.
Jim pays \$72 for sacks costing \$(m + 0.9) each.

(c) Find an expression, in terms of m, for the number of sacks Jim buys.

(d) Paulo buys 4 more sacks than Jim.
